tldr: Rekognition is a vision API and Weblate is a translation management platform; this is adopting Weblate for localization workflows, not a direct replacement.
why_switch: Weblate is open source and self-hostable, eliminating vendor lock-in.
Native Git integration keeps translations in version control.
Supports many file formats out of the box.
Better fit when the goal is managing translations across teams.
what_to_export: Existing translation files (po, json, xliff, properties, etc.)
User and translator list with roles
Sample screens per locale for QA
export_method: Collect translation files from your repo or current TMS in their native format. If translations don't yet exist, extract source strings from code into a supported file format (e.g., JSON, gettext PO, XLIFF). Consult Weblate official docs for the supported list of formats.
import_method: Set up a Weblate instance (self-hosted or hosted) and connect it to a Git repository containing the translation files. Configure components per file/locale layout, add languages, and invite translators. Weblate will commit translation changes back to Git automatically. Consult Weblate official docs for component configuration and CI integration.

concept_mapping.note: Translation workflow tool, not a runtime library.
Source of truth becomes Git, not S3.

effort: 1-3 days for setup and initial import
gotchas: Self-hosting Weblate adds infra responsibility (DB, backups, upgrades).
File format choice affects what features (plurals, context) you get.
Machine translation backends require their own API keys and billing.
Git history can grow large with many small translation commits.
Weblate does not replace a runtime i18n library — pair it with one (FormatJS, react-intl, next-intl, etc.).

email.subject: Migrating from Amazon Rekognition to Weblate
email.body:
Hi team,

Rekognition (vision) and Weblate (translation management) cover different needs. If translation workflow is the goal, the plan is to stand up Weblate, connect it to our Git repo, import existing translation files, and onboard translators. Estimated effort: 1-3 days for setup and initial import. We'll still need a runtime i18n library and, if applicable, a separate plan for any vision workloads.

Thanks
